intro_text:
Playa de las Teresitas is an artificial beach on the north coast of Tenerife, just north of the fishing village of San Andrés and about 8 kilometers from the center of Santa Cruz de Tenerife, the island's capital. It runs 1,300 meters long and roughly 80 meters wide, and almost none of it is native to the spot. The coast here is volcanic, and the sand it produces is black. The pale gold sand on Las Teresitas was shipped in.

The decision to build a beach here dates to a 1953 vote of the Santa Cruz city council, which wanted a proper bathing beach for the capital. After years of planning and approvals, work began in 1968 with a breakwater set about 150 meters offshore and running a full kilometer, designed to break the open Atlantic swell and hold imported sand in place against currents that would otherwise drag it away. In 1971 the council secured a 50-million-peseta loan and bought 150,000 cubic meters of sand — about 270,000 tons — from the El Aaiún region of what was then Spanish Sahara, in the far south. The sand crossed to Tenerife in roughly 5 million sacks aboard a ship called the Gopegui, operated by the phosphate firm Fosfatos de Bucraa, and was spread across the new bay in the first half of 1973. The finished beach opened to the public on 15 June 1973.

The breakwater did its job. It turned the bay into a shallow, near-current-free lagoon where the water shelves gently and stays calm even when the rest of Tenerife's north coast is taking Atlantic surf. That is the trade Las Teresitas makes: it is not a wild beach, and it was never meant to be. It is a constructed amenity for a city, backed by the steep dark ridges of the Anaga massif, one of the oldest and most biodiverse parts of the island. The contrast — gold sand, blue lagoon, black-green mountains — is the reason the beach photographs the way it does.

The sand has needed topping up. In 1998 another 2,800 tonnes of Saharan sand was added to replace material lost to the sea, at a cost of 400 million pesetas. The same period produced the beach's other defining story, which has nothing to do with geology: the Las Teresitas corruption case, in which the sale of adjoining land to the Santa Cruz council became one of the largest urban-planning scandals in the Canary Islands and ended, two decades later, in prison sentences confirmed by Spain's Supreme Court. The beach you swim at is calm, golden, and Blue Flag-certified; the land behind it was the subject of a criminal trial.
honest_reckoning_note:
Las Teresitas is honest about being made up, but the people who use it sometimes are not. The sand is not Tenerife's. It is Saharan, bought in 1971 and laid down in 1973, and the gentle lagoon it sits in exists only because a one-kilometer breakwater holds back the Atlantic that would otherwise be here. None of that is a flaw — it is a piece of municipal engineering that worked, and worked for fifty years. But it means the beach is a product, designed to a brief set by a city council, not a natural feature that happened to be beautiful.

The harder reckoning is the land behind the sand. In 1998 two investors bought the Las Teresitas parcels and three years later sold them to the Santa Cruz council for far more than the purchase price; the operation was later annulled in court and prosecuted as embezzlement and misconduct. The case ran for roughly two decades. Former mayor Miguel Zerolo was sentenced to seven years, the former urban-planning councilor to the same, and the businessmen and senior officials to multi-year terms, with the convictions confirmed by the Supreme Court. A separate tax-fraud ruling left the investment company owing 27.5 million euros on the Las Huertas sale. The golden beach is real and the swimming is good. The deal that controlled what gets built beside it was a crime.

day_in_time.dawn: At first light the bay is glassy behind its breakwater, the gold sand still cool, and the only people out are local walkers heading to the end of the stone mole for the view back across the water to Santa Cruz. The Anaga ridges behind the beach are still in shadow; the sun rises over the sea to the east.
day_in_time.midday: By midday the beach fills with santacruceros and families. The breakwater keeps the water flat and shallow, which is why this is the capital's family beach rather than a surf spot. Lifeguards work the marked zone; the chiringuitos along the back of the sand serve fish, beer, and the standard Canarian beach menu. Sun is intense — this is a subtropical Atlantic latitude — and shade is scarce on the open sand.
day_in_time.golden: Because the beach faces broadly north, it gets no head-on sunset, but the low light in the last hour before dusk warms the imported gold sand and throws the dark Anaga slopes into relief. This is the photographer's window and the quietest comfortable hour, as day-trippers head back toward the city.
day_in_time.night: After dark the beach empties; there is no resort strip on the sand itself. Life moves the short distance to San Andrés village, where the fish restaurants stay busy. The breakwater and the bay go dark and still, the lagoon flat under the Anaga ridgeline.

food_drink.name: Fresh fish in San Andrés
food_drink.description: San Andrés is a working fishing village immediately south of the beach, and its core specialty is whatever was landed locally — fish grilled or fried whole, eaten with papas arrugadas and mojo. The restaurants here, not the beach bars, are the reason locals come.
food_drink.where: San Andrés village, a few minutes' walk from the south end of the sand

food_drink.name: Papas arrugadas con mojo
food_drink.description: The Canarian staple: small potatoes boiled in heavily salted water until the skins wrinkle, served with mojo rojo (paprika, garlic, chili) or mojo verde (coriander or parsley). A regional dish, not a tourist invention, on every menu in San Andrés.
food_drink.where: Throughout San Andrés and the beach chiringuitos

food_drink.name: Chiringuito beach food
food_drink.description: The bars along the back of the beach serve the standard Canarian beach menu — fried fish, bocadillos, cold beer, soft drinks — at beachfront prices. Convenient rather than notable; the better eating is in the village behind.

timeline.title: The Castle of San Andrés is built
timeline.description: A defensive tower is constructed at San Andrés (most researchers date it to 1706) to guard the coast against pirate raids. Later partly destroyed when a ravine stream flooded, its leaning ruin still stands beside the village and is on Spain's Lista Roja heritage-risk list.

timeline.title: Santa Cruz votes to build a beach
timeline.description: The Santa Cruz de Tenerife city council decides to create an artificial bathing beach at Las Teresitas, where the natural coast is volcanic and lacks usable sand. Years of planning and approvals follow before any construction begins.

timeline.title: Breakwater construction begins
timeline.description: Work starts on the offshore breakwater, set about 150 meters from shore and running roughly one kilometer, built to break the Atlantic swell and hold imported sand against the currents.

timeline.title: Sand is bought from the Spanish Sahara
timeline.description: The council secures a 50-million-peseta loan and purchases 150,000 cubic meters of sand — about 270,000 tons — from the El Aaiún region of Spanish Sahara, packed in roughly 5 million sacks.

timeline.title: Las Teresitas opens
timeline.description: After the Saharan sand is shipped over on the Gopegui (operated by Fosfatos de Bucraa) and spread across the bay in the first half of the year, the finished 1,300-meter beach opens to the public on 15 June 1973.

timeline.title: Sand replenished, and the land is sold
timeline.description: Between April and November, 2,800 tonnes of fresh Saharan sand is added to replace material lost to the sea, at a cost of 400 million pesetas. The same year, investors buy the adjoining Las Teresitas land for 33 million euros — the start of the corruption case.

timeline.title: The land is resold to the council
timeline.description: The investors sell the Las Teresitas land to the Santa Cruz city council for 52.4 million euros, roughly tripling the purchase price three years earlier. The transaction is later annulled by the courts and prosecuted as one of the Canary Islands' largest urban-planning scandals.

timeline.title: Sale annulled; convictions confirmed
timeline.description: The 2001 sale is judicially annulled. Spain's Supreme Court ultimately confirms convictions: former mayor Miguel Zerolo and the former urban-planning councilor sentenced to seven years for embezzlement and misconduct, with the investors and senior officials receiving multi-year terms.

zones.name: The main golden strand
zones.character: The 1,300-meter stretch of imported Saharan sand, gold against the volcanic coast. Calm, shallow, and shelving gently behind the breakwater. The capital's family beach, busiest in its central and southern sections near the facilities.
zones.notes: Lifeguarded in a marked zone; Blue Flag certified. Shade is limited on the open sand.

zones.name: The offshore breakwater
zones.character: The stone mole built from 1968, set about 150 meters offshore and running roughly a kilometer, the engineering that makes the lagoon possible. Walkable to its end for views back across the water to Santa Cruz.
zones.best_for: Walking, views over the bay and city
zones.notes: The structural reason the water inside stays flat; without it the Atlantic would reclaim the sand.

zones.name: San Andrés village
zones.character: The working fishing village at the south end of the beach, older than the beach itself, with the leaning ruin of the 18th-century Castle of San Andrés and the cluster of fish restaurants that draw locals.
zones.best_for: Eating, the village and castle ruin
zones.notes: The eating-and-evening center; the beach has facilities but no resort strip.

zones.name: The Anaga backdrop
zones.character: The steep, dark ridges of the Anaga massif rising directly behind the beach, one of the oldest and most biodiverse parts of Tenerife and a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. The visual contrast that defines every photograph of the bay.
zones.notes: Volcanic green-black mountains behind gold sand and blue water.

landmarks.name: The offshore breakwater
landmarks.description: Stone breakwater set roughly 150 meters offshore and running about one kilometer, begun in 1968 to break the Atlantic swell and retain the imported sand. The structure that converts an exposed volcanic coast into a calm bathing lagoon.

landmarks.name: Castle of San Andrés
landmarks.description: Defensive tower at San Andrés, most often dated to 1706, built against pirate raids on the Santa Cruz coast. Partly destroyed when a ravine stream flooded; its leaning stone ruin survives beside the village and sits on Spain's Lista Roja heritage-risk register.

landmarks.name: Anaga massif
landmarks.description: The ancient volcanic mountain range forming the northeastern tip of Tenerife and the backdrop to the beach. A UNESCO Biosphere Reserve since 2015, among the oldest and most biodiverse terrain on the island.

cultural_refs.title: The Las Teresitas corruption case
cultural_refs.description: One of the largest urban-planning scandals in the Canary Islands, centered on the sale of land beside the beach to the Santa Cruz council for 52.4 million euros after a 33-million-euro purchase three years earlier. The sale was annulled and prosecuted, ending in Supreme Court-confirmed prison sentences for the former mayor, a councilor, and the investors.

cultural_refs.title: A European beach made of Saharan sand
cultural_refs.description: Las Teresitas is frequently cited in travel and geography writing as the case of a European beach built entirely from desert sand shipped across the Atlantic — roughly 270,000 tons from the Spanish Sahara — rather than from the volcanic coast it sits on.

things_to_know.label: The sand is imported
things_to_know.note: Tenerife's natural coast here is black volcanic rock; the gold sand was shipped from the Spanish Sahara in 1973 and topped up in 1998.

things_to_know.label: Calm water by design
things_to_know.note: The bay is shallow and near-current-free because a one-kilometer breakwater holds back the Atlantic — this is the capital's family swimming beach, not a surf spot.

things_to_know.label: Eat in the village
things_to_know.note: The fish restaurants of San Andrés, just south of the sand, are the local draw; the beach chiringuitos are convenient but unremarkable.

things_to_know.label: Blue Flag, lifeguarded
things_to_know.note: Las Teresitas holds Blue Flag status and is lifeguarded within a marked zone; shade on the open sand is limited and the UV is high.

access_note: About 8 km north of central Santa Cruz de Tenerife, reached by car or by TITSA city buses (lines from the Santa Cruz bus station, roughly 25-35 minutes). The nearest airport is Tenerife Norte (TFN), about 15.6 km away. There is a paid beach car park; arrive early on summer weekends.
spike_explainer:
The simplest true sentence about Playa de las Teresitas is that the sand is not from Tenerife. The island's north coast is volcanic, and the beaches it makes on its own are black or dark grey. The pale gold sand on Las Teresitas came from a desert on the African mainland, and it is there because a city decided it should be.

**The brief.** In 1953 the Santa Cruz de Tenerife council voted to build itself a proper bathing beach. The capital sat on a working volcanic coast with no good sand of its own, and the council wanted an amenity — a wide, golden, calm-water beach close to the city. It took years of planning and approvals before anything was built. The project that resulted was not a restoration of a natural beach; it was the construction of one from scratch, to specification.

**The engineering.** Two problems had to be solved: the Atlantic, and the sand. The Atlantic was handled first. From 1968 a stone breakwater was built about 150 meters offshore and run for roughly a kilometer, set to break the open swell and turn the exposed bay into a sheltered lagoon. Without it, any sand placed here would be dragged straight back out to sea. With it, the water inside shelves gently and stays flat — the reason Las Teresitas became the family beach of the capital rather than a surf coast.

**The sand.** Then came the fill. In 1971 the council took out a 50-million-peseta loan and bought 150,000 cubic meters of sand, weighing about 270,000 tons, from the El Aaiún region of what was then Spanish Sahara, the Spanish colony on the African coast to the south. The sand was packed into roughly 5 million sacks and carried across to Tenerife aboard a ship called the Gopegui, operated by the phosphate company Fosfatos de Bucraa. Over the first half of 1973 it was spread across the bay behind the new breakwater. The beach opened to the public on 15 June 1973.

**The upkeep.** A built beach is a maintained beach. The sea still works at the sand, and in 1998 the council added 2,800 more tonnes of Saharan sand to replace what had been lost, at a cost of 400 million pesetas. The gold color that reads as natural in every photograph is, in fact, a managed stock of imported material, replenished as it depletes — and, in the early years, accompanied by some unintended imports: red ants and scorpions carried over in the sand.

**The honesty of it.** Las Teresitas is sometimes described as if it were a found wonder, a tropical beach improbably attached to a volcanic island. It is better understood as good civic engineering: a city looked at a coast that would not give it the beach it wanted, and built that beach anyway, out of a breakwater and a desert shipped in sacks. The result is genuinely pleasant — 1,300 meters of warm gold sand, flat clear water, the dark Anaga ridges behind. What it is not is natural. The most interesting thing about the beach is exactly the thing the postcard leaves out: that it is a product, dated 1973, with a purchase order behind it.

key_facts.label: Corruption case
key_facts.value: Ex-mayor Zerolo sentenced to 7 years; sale annulled 2018

margin_notes.text: Tenerife's own beaches are volcanic and run black or dark grey. The gold of Las Teresitas is mineralogically foreign to the island — quartz-and-feldspar desert sand from the African mainland, laid over a basaltic coast that produces nothing like it.

The sand came from El Aaiún in Spanish Sahara in 1971, while it was still a Spanish colony; Spain withdrew in 1975-76. The beach is a small artifact of the late Spanish empire, bought before the territory was given up.

The numbers were large for 1971: a 50-million-peseta loan for the sand alone, carried in roughly 5 million sacks aboard the Gopegui. A 1998 top-up of 2,800 tonnes cost another 400 million pesetas — a built beach is a recurring expense.

The breakwater makes the swimming but flattens the ecology: the bay is a managed lagoon, not a natural intertidal shore. The Anaga massif behind it, by contrast, is a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve and among Tenerife's oldest, most biodiverse terrain.

The land scandal outlasted the men who started it. The 2001 sale to the council was annulled in 2018, and the Supreme Court confirmed seven-year sentences for the former mayor and a councilor. The swimming was never in question; the planning around it was criminal.

reckoning_pullquote: The golden beach is real and the swimming is good. The deal that controlled what gets built beside it was a crime.
